title: Data Markets and Privacy Externalities
payoff_functions: Platform objective: minimize \(\sum_{i\in V} \mathbb{E}[(\hat x_i(S)-X_i)^2-\sigma_i^2+p_i]\). User i payoff: \(u_i(a_i,a_{-i},p_i)= p_i - v_i I_i(a_i=1,a_{-i})\) if \(a_i=1\), and \(u_i(a_i,a_{-i},p_i)= -v_i I_i(a_i=0,a_{-i})\) if \(a_i=0\). Social surplus: \(U(a,p)+\sum_i u_i(a,p)=\sum_i (1-v_i)I_i(a)\). Equilibrium price sustaining action profile a: if \(a_i=1\), \(p_i^a = v_i(\sigma_i^2-I_i(a_i=0,a_{-i}))^2/((\sigma_i^2+1)-I_i(a_i=0,a_{-i}))\); if \(a_i=0\), \(p_i^a=0\).
key_mechanisms: The core mechanism is that one user’s data disclosure leaks information about other users, reducing the privacy cost they perceive from sharing and thereby lowering the price needed to induce further disclosure. This informational externality depresses data prices below the social value of privacy, causing excessive sharing relative to the first best. Competition among platforms does not fix this distortion and can worsen welfare because platforms still price data against the private marginal incentives, not the external harm imposed on others.
